title: Did medieval people love their children?  - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: The popular belief that medieval parents were emotionally distant from their children, associated with historian Philippe Ariès, has been thoroughly refuted by later scholarship such as Nicholas Orme's Medieval Children. Demographic, economic, and documentary evidence shows medieval English family life and parental affection closely resembled our own.

insights.claim: French historian Philippe Ariès argued in the 1960s that medieval childhood was profoundly remote from ours, claiming children were seen as defective or incomplete adults and not loved as we love ours.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Names the specific scholarly source of a widely held popular belief, making it possible to test and dispute rather than treat as folk wisdom.
insights.quote: a French historian who argued in the 1960s that medieval childhood was profoundly remote in these ways
insights.locator: As I grew older, I came to associate this idea

insights.claim: Ariès's thesis about emotionally remote medieval childhood was never fully accepted in academia yet became vastly influential on the wider public.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Illustrates how an idea can dominate popular imagination while being rejected by specialists, a caution about the gap between scholarship and public belief.
insights.quote: Ariès’s work never attained complete acceptance in academia, but it was vastly influential on the wider public.
insights.locator: Ariès’s work never attained complete acceptance

insights.claim: Historian Nicholas Orme, whose Medieval Children is the leading English study, states there is nothing to be said for Ariès's view and that it is mistaken in substance, not merely in detail.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Provides the authoritative counter-evidence that overturns the prevailing mental model of a cold medieval family.
insights.quote: It cannot be overemphasised that there is nothing to be said for Ariès’s view
insights.locator: Nicholas Orme’s Medieval Children is the leading study

insights.claim: Most medieval English people had only three or four children, marrying late in their mid or late twenties, contrary to the assumption that medieval families were very large.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Corrects a common misconception and reframes medieval family structure as far closer to modern norms than imagined.
insights.quote: most medieval people had only three or four children, somewhat more among the rich and somewhat fewer among the very poor
insights.locator: It is sometimes imagined that medieval people had a great many children

insights.claim: In medieval England about 25 percent of children died in their first year and another 15 percent by their tenth, leaving the median child growing up with one sibling and two parents in their thirties or forties, just like the median English child today.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies mortality while showing the surviving household structure was strikingly similar to the present, undercutting the sense of medieval otherness.
insights.quote: The median English child thus grew up in a home with one sibling and two parents in their thirties or forties, being in this respect just like the median English child today.
insights.locator: About 25 percent of children died in their first year

themes.theme: Medieval attitudes to abortion and the fetus

insights.claim: Medieval people believed early-term fetuses were alive but unconscious, like vegetables, and lacked souls, so early-term abortion was considered sinful but not homicide and was not punishable at common law.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reveals a more nuanced medieval moral-legal framework on abortion than the assumption of blanket prohibition, with implications for the deep history of the abortion debate.
insights.quote: Medieval people did not think that early-term fetuses had souls, believing them to be alive but unconscious, like vegetables.
insights.locator: Abortion was considered sinful, but the situation was more complicated

insights.claim: The distinction between early-term abortion (not homicide) and late-term abortion (a kind of homicide, but still not normally punished at common law) persisted until the nineteenth century.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows a legal framework on abortion that endured for centuries, reframing modern debates as departures from a long-standing gradualist tradition.
insights.quote: This distinction persisted until the nineteenth century.
insights.locator: Late-term abortion was thought to be a kind of homicide

insights.claim: Medieval parents made immense sacrifices for their children while expecting little in return: feeding a child consumed about 15 percent of average income and parents often supported children throughout adulthood.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The material cost of raising children undercuts the theory that parents stayed emotionally detached as a coping mechanism.
insights.quote: Feeding a child consumed about 15 percent of average income, and parents often continued to support children throughout their adulthood.
insights.locator: Medieval parents made immense sacrifices for their children

insights.claim: Children were typically sent away at age 13 or 14 for apprenticeship or service just when they could become a net economic asset, and parents generally paid for the apprenticeships rather than receiving remitted income.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Demonstrates parents invested in children beyond any economic self-interest, evidence against a purely transactional view of medieval family bonds.
insights.quote: at just the age they might become a net economic asset – 13 or 14 – they were usually sent away to acquire skills and savings through apprenticeship or working in service
insights.locator: Older children often performed light work at home

insights.claim: Grateful medieval children sometimes supported their parents in old age, but this does not appear to have been the norm.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Counters any assumption that medieval parenting was primarily a self-interested investment in future old-age support.
insights.quote: Grateful children sometimes supported their parents in old age, but again this does not seem to have been the norm.
insights.locator: They may have remitted some of this income

insights.claim: When Katherine, the disabled and non-speaking three-year-old daughter of Henry III and Eleanor of Provence, died, both parents were heartbroken and the chronicler Matthew Parris recorded that Queen Eleanor fell sick with grief and could find no relief in medicine nor human consolation.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A documented instance of profound parental grief directly refutes the claim that high child mortality bred emotional indifference.
insights.quote: and could find no relief in medicine nor human consolation
insights.locator: The fifth child of Henry III and Eleanor of Provence

insights.claim: Contemporary texts record parental and childhood joy: an Oxford schoolbook of the 1490s describes a schoolboy reunited with his parents weeping for joy, and the thirteenth-century treatise Ancrene Wisse imagined Jesus playing hide-and-seek with a believer as a mother does with her child.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Positive emotional evidence, including affectionate parent-child play used as a religious metaphor, shows warmth was a recognized and celebrated part of medieval family life.
insights.quote: we wept for joy, each to other

insights.claim: Orme concludes that medieval people were essentially ourselves five hundred or a thousand years ago, not the strange and cold figures the popular imagination has pictured.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reframes the past as continuous with the present rather than radically alien, challenging narratives of deep human discontinuity.
insights.quote: I believe they were ourselves, five hundred or a thousand years ago
insights.locator: The medieval people who emerge from Orme’s book

insights.claim: When a scholar makes exciting claims that some class of human beings is incomprehensibly unlike us, we should be skeptical and ask whether the claim is even plausible, just as the Ariès hypothesis fails the test of whether children could survive medieval hardship without devoted parents.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Offers a general heuristic for evaluating dramatic historical or social-science revisionism: dramatic claims of human otherness deserve heightened scrutiny.
insights.quote: Next time a scholar makes exciting claims about how some class of human beings is incomprehensibly unlike us, we would do well to ask similar questions.
insights.locator: Perhaps we should ask why the Ariès hypothesis
